item.title: Continuous Learnig
item.description:
TLDR

Keep learning and practicing all sorts of skills and not just technical. What people call "soft skills" are very important to your career path

There is always artciles and people talking about how in order to be uptodate in our industry, we need so many hours everyday for learning.
Keep updated on the new technologies, learn a new language, depth and breadth.
I understood and accepted that, and tried to keep up with every new thing around, while deepning my knowldge on the stuff I work on.
To keep learning is not particularly easy, it requires a lot of energy and perseverance. With every new tech we learn we get out of our comfort zone.

Every now and then we are confronted with new situations, that challenge us, not technical. All the repository of tech we learned everyday won’t help.
What have I missed ?
Why am I unable to deal with that?
It seems to me that I focused my "learning" in one direction and missed all the rest.
Some people call them the "soft skills", I call them "skills".
Labelling these skills "soft" makes them seem less important, while they are very important and even critical for my career path.
Empathy, listening, anger management, self control, pitching, just to name a few.
The same practices of learning tech skills apply, you need to learn and practice. If I learn a new language say GOLANG and I don’t practice it enough, I will forgrt it or at least i would struggle everytime i try to do anything with GOLANG.
The same with empathy or self control, if I don’t practice those then I will either not use them when needed or strugle everytime i need them.
It will not be easy to practice self control if I only spend my time in my comfort zone. I need to get out and expose myself to new situations, take on new responsibilities/roles, look more at my team and analyze our interactions to find where I could have acted differently. Find the situations/people I try to avoid and actually face those.
item.dc:creator: Ramez Hanna

item.title: Dealing with team rejection
item.description:
TLDR

Team needs to establish healthy relationships between its members based on trust and communications.

A few weeks in my new job in Paris after just moving in from Helsinki, my manager tells me in our biweekly 1 on 1 meeting

"You have an issue with the team, nobody seems to want to work with you"
Heart beats accelerate, blood rushes through my face and the urge respond violently to such a claim escalates.
A deep breath and a few seconds later i respond supercially calmly
"could you give me more details, more facts or anything to helpme understand why"

First rule when dealing with negative feedback, always take a deep breath before reacting.

At that moment all sorts of thoughts are rushing in my mind, I was not even fully attentive to my managers explanations. So again I take a deep breath and refocus on understanding the "WHY" of the feedback.+ Regardless of the details of the discussion that followed, the conclusion was: My manager got this sentiment from each and everyone and he does not have more details. I am all alone in this.

I have (or at least i thought so) 2 ways of dealing with this.

  • Engage my child refuse to accept that feedback, retaliate and give negative feedback on each one of them.

  • Engage my adult try to understand WHY and HOW to remedy that.

I decided to act like an adult.

I reserved a 30 minutes slot for each team member (5 excluding my manager) and met with each of them with only 1 question to ask

  • Why do you feel that you don’t want to work with me?

And the intention of establishing a trust relationship. Ready to admit my mistakes and resolve any issues.

After the round of 1on1 meetings, I came to a few reasons

  • I do act like a jerk sometimes, specially that I have not worked in a team for a long time.

  • I act in a different way than they are used to

  • They don’t really know me

The reason that stood out was about me acting in a different way that they are used to. For example when I approach someone for a question, I don’t start by saying hi or anysort of intro, but i do go straight to the question. Another example I don’t say good morning to everyone when I arrive in the morning.
I do say hi when i arrive but i just throw it to the group (UDP way), I prefer to be direct when i talk work. That’s just how I am and how i learned to act while living in finland for the past 8 years.

These some simple examples among other things and they all boil down to lack of communication and cultural difference.

Now that the WHY is known and communication established with the team, the rest is easy. Well relatively easy and requires vigilance and hard work.
Relationships are not made by meeting once, I had taken the task of making sure I communicate with everyone in the team make sure all potential issues are resolved early and continuous two-way feedback. Yes I also had to work on my habits and flaws.

IMHO I don’t think I would have resolved the issue by only working on my attitude without openly communicating with my team. I beleive a big part was not about my attitude. How many times have you had a colleague who had annoying traits but yet it did not affect your work relations.

A team is not afraid of conflicts. The team uses trust and communication to deal with conflicts
item.dc:creator: Ramez Hanna

item.title: lid-close enlightenment 0.20 fedora
item.description:
A problem that has been anying me for a while now, Enlightenment 0.20 on fedora cannot detect the lid-close event and hence lid-close does not do anything. What I want is on lid-close blank and lock screen, this serves as a quick screen lock and also save battery. == why it doesn’t work fedora does not install and enable/start acpid by default. without acpid enlightenment does not know about power events. in Fedora systemd is the system that handles those and sends notifs to the DM, while other DMs like gnome know how to handle systemd events, enlightenment does not

solution

install and enable acpid

[user~]\>$ dnf install acpid
[user~]\>$ systemctl start acpid
[user~]\>$ systemdctl enable acpid

reload enlightenment using the ctrl-alt-end now it should be able to detect lid-close events. from the acpi bindings settings pabne set the desired action

I am still unable to set it to blank the screen.
item.dc:creator: Ramez Hanna

item.title: Testing chef cookbooks the dirty way
item.description:
I work with chef on a weekly basis and a lot of times i need to do modify cookbooks that already run well. Good practices say you must test your code first, so I do that. But kitchen is sooo slooowww. sometines i just can’t tolerate it. And other times you hit a bug and you are not sure why it works in kitchen but fails on the server. In those cases you want to be able to test faster the changes you are doing. Hence I use this dirty way to test my cookbook changes

Say I a modify the recipe 'config.rb' of cookbook 'service'. here is what i do

cd /var/chef/cache/
# modify the recipe in the cache
# runc chef client from cache
chef-client --once --local-mode -o service::config

TADA you run modified code on the server.
item.dc:creator: Ramez Hanna

item.title: Deploying openAM 12 on mesos
item.description:
Background

OpenAM provides open source Authentication, Authorization, Entitlement and Federation software. We chose it as part of our internal SSO platform. The documentation of openAM goes into a lot of details about the installation that I don’t need to go through here again.
openAM 12 was not deigned as a stateless app, and is not meant to be runnig on an elastic env like mesos.

The purpose of this doc is to highlight the main pain points and challenges in deploying openAM on a mesos cluster

References

  • https://backstage.forgerock.com/#!/docs/openam/12.0.0/

  • http://azlabs.blogspot.fr/2015/08/openam-12-sessions.html

  • http://blogs.forgerock.org/petermajor/2015/08/sessions/

Constraints

  • Mesos cluster only runs stateless non-persistent apps

  • Marathon framework to manage long running apps on Mesos

  • Need to run multiple instances for HA

Setup

1. External config store

Configure openAM to use openDJ for storing its configuation.
Use the openam-configurator tool to push that config file.

By default openAM uses its own embeded backend for storing its config and dumping all that on disk in the openAM home as seen in the config below BASE_DIR+ The first time to run this tool it will create the BASE_DIR and connect to the backend to write configs.
In subsequent runs of the configurator tool it will connect to the backend and check that there is a config and only write the BASE_DIR part without changing any configs. From here on configs will be handled by sooadm tool or the webUI (console). There are 2 configs in that context

# Server properties
SERVER_URL=http://${HOSTNAME}:${PORT0}
DEPLOYMENT_URI=/openam
BASE_DIR=/tmp/openam
locale=en_US
PLATFORM_LOCALE=en_US
AM_ENC_KEY='some_random_string'
ADMIN_PWD='a_good_pass'
AMLDAPUSERPASSWD='another_good_pass'
COOKIE_DOMAIN=.example.com
ACCEPT_LICENSES=true
# External configuration data store
DATA_STORE=dirServer (1)
DIRECTORY_SSL=SIMPLE
DIRECTORY_SERVER=${OPENDJ_SERVER}
DIRECTORY_PORT=${OPENDJ_PORT}
DIRECTORY_ADMIN_PORT=${OPENDJ_ADMIN_PORT}
ROOT_SUFFIX=$OPENAM_SUFFIX
DS_DIRMGRDN=${OPENDJ_USER}
DS_DIRMGRPASSWD=${OPENDJ_PASS}
# External User store
USERSTORE_TYPE=LDAPv3ForOpenDS
USERSTORE_SSL=SIMPLE
USERSTORE_HOST=${OPENDJ_SERVER}
USERSTORE_PORT=${OPENDJ_PORT}
USERSTORE_SUFFIX=ou=people,dc=example, dc=com
USERSTORE_MGRDN=${OPENDJ_USER}
USERSTORE_PASSWD=${OPENDJ_PASS}
# Site config
LB_SITE_NAME=example
LB_PRIMARY_URL=http://sso.example.com:80/openam
LB_SESSION_HA_SFO=true (2)
1 Storing config in openDJ
2 enable session failover
There is a whole section in the docs on how to prepare the external backend for openAM to use it, make sure to follow it correctly

2. Clustering

Clustering in openAM is a topic that i did not dive deep into, but a few points to note.

  • Every server registers to the cluster with its hostname:port

  • When a server is down it does not leave the cluster or get deleted

  • Sessions are stored in memory on the host, so server down = session lost

2.1. CTS (centralized session store)

CTS is the means for openAM to be able to share its sessions among the hosts of the cluster.
It can use openDJ to store its sessions, hence allowing a user authenticated on one host to be able to resume on another server.
This is the most important part of the setup

It solves 2 problems

  • session sharing

  • and session persistence across reboots

This is again well documented in the docs, and pay special attention to the preparations on the openDJ side.

2.2. Scaling

As mentioned before, openAM does not clean its list of hosts in the cluster and as mesos would launch each time a new instance, the need to clean old instances becomes evident.
There are 2 solutions for that, either in the script that launches openam we catch TERM signals and remove the exiting host form the cluster (which is not so easy), or take a hacky way to ensure instances always have the same name.+ Here is how I achieved that.
This is the JSON file to launch on marathon.

{
  "id": "openam",
  "cmd":"mkdir jetty && tar xzf jetty-9.3.3.v20150827.tar.gz -C jetty --strip-components=1 && mv openam-server-12.0.0-criteo-15.war /tmp/openam.war && mkdir openam-configurator && unzip openam-configurator-12.0.0.zip -d openam-configurator && bash -x startup.sh",
  "cpus": 2,
  "mem": 1024,
  "env": {
    "OPENDJ_SERVER":"opendj.example.com",
    "OPENDJ_USER":"cn=admin",
    "OPENDJ_PASS":"password",
    "OPENAM_SUFFIX":"dc=openam,dc=example,dc=com",
    "JAVA_HOME": "/usr/lib/jvm/jre-1.8.0/bin"
  },
  "instances": 2,
  "ports": [
    33333 (1)
  ]
  "requirePorts": true, (2)
  "constraints":[
    ["hostname","LIKE","slave0[1,2]"] (3)
  ],
  "healthChecks": [{
    "path": "/auth/isAlive.jsp",
    "protocol": "HTTP",
    "portIndex": 0,
    "gracePeriodSeconds": 300,
    "intervalSeconds": 60,
    "timeoutSeconds": 20,
    "maxConsecutiveFailures": 3,
    "ignoreHttp1xx": false
  }],
  "uris": [
    "http://fileserver.example.com/openam-configurator-12.0.0.zip",
    "http://fileserver.example.com/jetty.tar.gz",
    "http://fileserver.example.com/openam.war",
    "http://fileserver.example.com/startup.sh"
  ]
}
1 Specify the port number
2 Enforces the port number defined in 1
3 Limits the pool to 2 slaves

This way we always have 2 servers slave01:33333 and slave02:33333

3. Starting

Now that opeAM needs to run its config everytime it starts I use the startup.sh for that.
startup.sh is a bash script that start openam on jetty and sends it to background, waits for it to be fully started by looking at the log file for Server:main: Started, then it will launch the openam-configurator tool to configure openAM.

# Start jetty
cd $JETTY_HOME
${JAVA_HOME}/java  -server -jar start.jar -Djetty.http.port=${PORT0} ${JAVA_OPTS}> /tmp/openam.log 2>&1 &
until [ "$(grep -q 'Server:main: Started @' /tmp/openam.log && echo $?)" == "0" ]
 do
  sleep 5
done
cd ../

Then after that it writes the config file above and runs the configurator

${JAVA_HOME}/java -jar openam-configurator/openam-configurator-tool-12.0.0.jar -f /tmp/openam.conf
#run again to work around a bug solved in 12.0.1 but we have 12.0.0
if [ "$?" != "0" ]; then
  ${JAVA_HOME}/java -jar openam-configurator/openam-configurator-tool-12.0.0.jar -f /tmp/openam.conf
  [ "$?" != "0" ] && exit 1
fi

then at the end let the script simulate a daemon

while true; do sleep 60; done

This way the script remains running and mesos keeps the task, I don’t worry about the terminating it correctly when mesos is attempting to kill the task as mesos will send a TERM to all tasks in that that container anyway.

Now openAM is ready.

Next step is to configure your SSO solution
item.dc:creator: Ramez Hanna
